items.title: # Tip 1
items.content: Start every request by giving the model a clear role, like "You are a friendly tutor" or "Act as a market analyst." The role sets the tone and limits, so the AI answers in the right voice and depth, making your output more useful and on-point.

items.title: # Tip 2
items.content: Combine role prompting with a short task description. For example: "You are a travel planner. Suggest a 3-day itinerary for a family with kids in Tokyo." The role frames expertise, while the task tells the AI exactly what to produce, reducing vague replies.

items.title: # Tip 3
items.content: Refresh the role when the conversation shifts. If you move from brainstorming ideas to checking facts, say "Now you are a fact-checker. Verify these statements." Updating the role keeps the model focused and prevents mix-ups.

items.title: # Tip 1
items.content: When you ask an AI, include exact details like the format, length, and style you want. Instead of saying "write a story," say "write a 200-word sci-fi story in first person, with a twist ending." Clear specifics guide the model to give you exactly what you need.

items.title: # Tip 2
items.content: Add context that narrows the scope. If you need a marketing tagline, mention the product, target audience, and tone (e.g., "fun, eco-friendly"). This prevents vague answers and helps the AI generate copy that fits your real situation.

items.title: # Tip 3
items.content: Use concrete examples to illustrate your request. Instead of "explain AI," say "explain AI to a 10-year-old using a soccer game analogy." Specific examples shape the response, making it easier to understand and apply.
